stem: A neonate presents with progressive respiratory distress. CXR shows a hyperinflated left upper lobe with mediastinal shift. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Congenital lobar emphysema (CLE) presents with progressive air trapping in an affected lobe, most commonly the left upper lobe (42%), followed by right middle lobe (35%). The hyperinflated lobe compresses adjacent lung and shifts the mediastinum. Caused by deficient bronchial cartilage leading to a ball-valve mechanism. Treatment: surgical lobectomy for symptomatic cases. [StatPearls NBK538347]

feedback.correct: Correct! Hyperinflated single lobe + mediastinal shift = CLE. Left upper lobe is most common.
feedback.incorrect: CLE = progressive air trapping in one lobe from deficient bronchial cartilage. LUL most common.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: What is the underlying mechanism of congenital lobar emphysema?

choices.text: Deficiency of bronchial cartilage causing air trapping

explanation: CLE results from deficient or absent bronchial cartilage, creating a ball-valve mechanism — air enters on inspiration but cannot exit on expiration, causing progressive lobar hyperinflation. Other causes include mucus plugging or extrinsic compression. The affected lobe is hyperinflated but not destroyed (unlike emphysema in adults). [StatPearls NBK538347]

feedback.correct: Correct! Deficient bronchial cartilage → ball-valve air trapping → progressive lobar hyperinflation.
feedback.incorrect: CLE = cartilage deficiency → air trapping (ball-valve). Not related to surfactant or diaphragm defects.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: The laryngotracheal groove develops from which embryonic structure during week 4?

choices.text: Ventral wall of the foregut endoderm

explanation: The respiratory system develops from the ventral wall of the foregut. During week 4, the laryngotracheal groove appears in the ventral foregut endoderm, which elongates to form the laryngotracheal diverticulum (lung bud). The tracheoesophageal septum then separates the ventral respiratory bud from the dorsal esophagus. Failure of this separation → tracheoesophageal fistula. [StatPearls NBK544372]

feedback.correct: Correct! The respiratory system buds from the ventral foregut endoderm during week 4.
feedback.incorrect: The laryngotracheal groove develops from ventral foregut endoderm. Failure of septation → TEF.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: Failure of the tracheoesophageal septum to properly divide the foregut results in which congenital anomaly?
explanation: The tracheoesophageal (TE) septum divides the foregut into the ventral trachea and dorsal esophagus. Incomplete septation → tracheoesophageal fistula (TEF), most commonly with esophageal atresia (EA). The most common type (85%) has proximal EA with distal TEF. Presents with polyhydramnios, inability to pass NG tube, choking with first feeds. Associated with VACTERL. [StatPearls NBK544372]

feedback.correct: Correct! Failed TE septum → TEF/EA. Most common: proximal atresia + distal fistula (Type C, 85%).
feedback.incorrect: TE septum failure → tracheoesophageal fistula. VACTERL association. Polyhydramnios + can't pass NG tube.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A fetus has a large cystic lung mass detected on prenatal ultrasound. Postnatal CT confirms a multicystic lesion. What feature distinguishes CPAM from pulmonary sequestration?

choices.text: CPAM communicates with the tracheobronchial tree; sequestration has systemic arterial supply
CPAM has systemic arterial supply; sequestration communicates with airways
CPAM only occurs in the right lung

explanation: CPAM (congenital pulmonary airway malformation) communicates with the tracheobronchial tree and has normal pulmonary blood supply. Pulmonary sequestration is non-communicating lung tissue with an anomalous systemic arterial supply (typically from the aorta). CPAM types: Type 1 (large cysts, most common, best prognosis), Type 2 (small cysts), Type 3 (solid/microcystic). [StatPearls NBK551664]

feedback.correct: Correct! CPAM connects to airways with pulmonary blood supply. Sequestration has systemic arterial supply and no airway connection.
feedback.incorrect: CPAM = airway communication + pulmonary blood supply. Sequestration = no airway connection + systemic (aortic) blood supply.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A child has recurrent left lower lobe pneumonias. CT angiography reveals an aberrant systemic artery arising from the thoracic aorta supplying the affected tissue. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Pulmonary sequestration is non-functioning lung tissue with anomalous systemic arterial supply (from aorta) and no connection to the tracheobronchial tree. Intralobar (75%) shares visceral pleura with normal lung and presents with recurrent infections, usually LLL. Extralobar (25%) has its own pleura, presents in neonates. Diagnosis: CT angiography showing systemic arterial supply. Treatment: surgical resection. [StatPearls NBK532955]

feedback.correct: Correct! Systemic arterial supply + recurrent LLL infections = intralobar pulmonary sequestration.
feedback.incorrect: Aberrant aortic blood supply to non-communicating lung tissue = sequestration. Intralobar type causes recurrent pneumonias.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: What is the key difference between intralobar and extralobar pulmonary sequestration?

choices.text: Intralobar has its own pleura; extralobar shares pleura
Intralobar shares pleura with normal lung; extralobar has its own pleural covering
Intralobar presents in neonates; extralobar in adults
Intralobar has pulmonary blood supply; extralobar has systemic

explanation: Intralobar sequestration (75%) shares visceral pleura with adjacent normal lung, presents later with recurrent infections, and has venous drainage to pulmonary veins. Extralobar (25%) has its own separate pleural investment, presents in neonates (often with other anomalies), and drains to systemic veins (IVC, azygos). Both have anomalous systemic arterial supply. [StatPearls NBK532955]

feedback.correct: Correct! Intralobar shares pleura (75%, recurrent infections). Extralobar has own pleura (25%, neonatal).
feedback.incorrect: Intralobar = shared pleura, older presentation. Extralobar = own pleura, neonatal presentation.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use
